name: Vhasia
description:
Vhasia

Key Demographics: Humans

Capital City: Lence

Entanglements

To the West:
Acasia – Border raids across the Belworth define the seasons. The Curse of Acasia does not respect the river; neither do the routiers.
To the East:
Viterra – The Fenwood wound is still raw; the Warrior Queen's eyes watch the mountain passes. The Belworth is a border written in blood.
To the North:
Aeler / Ubral – High passes funnel mercenaries and exiles into the fractured sun-kingdom. The mountain holds are a sieve, not a wall.
To the South:
Thepyrgos (via Viterra) – Synod law and the Chain-Lanterns rarely venture this far north, but their scholars collect Vhasian ballads. The ninth verse is always censored.

Quick Reference

Tagline: The sun shattered at Lence. The pieces are still falling. Which shadow will catch you?

Cardinal-Regent of the Broken Sun (Elite):

“The sun may be fractured, but our authority is absolute within our domains. Every coin minted here carries our blessing, and every law written bears our seal—the rest is just politics. The rest is also ash. Ask the Fenwoods. They made the ash, and they left us to choke on it. But remember: the first crack came not from their swords, but from the marriage beds where a Vhasian queen bore sons to two Viterran kings—and the sons who wondered whose blood truly ran in their veins. Chivalry? A thin veneer. Scratch it, and you find the iron beneath.”

Road Warden (Commoner):

“I've served under three banners on this same road. The only constant is that someone always wants to collect tolls, oaths, the dead. The Fenwoods burned Lence ten years ago, marching from the Belworth like storm-clouds. Now every duke with a purse thinks he's king. The Queen-Mother weaves in the west, Valdais sharpens his lance in Adria, and the Two Crowns count their silver. Me? I just want to get home before the snows bury the graves. And if you hear a cantor singing the Planctus Armatus, stop and listen. That's the only honest history left—especially the verse about the queen who married twice, bore sons to both kings, and left the realm wondering which throne was hers by right, and which by rumor.”

Genre / Mood: Courtly intrigue, chevauchée warfare, fractured legitimacy, the tragedy of chivalry—a kingdom that cannot mourn because mourning would mean admitting the old world is dead. Beneath the gilt and the heraldry, chivalry is a thin veneer; the iron of siege and sack shows through at every edge.

Starting Location: The Pont-du-Tithe, its golden arches catching fire in the sunset as pilgrims chant from both banks. A Duchy Constable in splendid armour guards the toll gate. “The chevauchée burns to the east,” he says, gesturing toward the Belworth. “Two Crowns march to meet it. Whose seal do you carry? Speak quickly—the levy waits for no one. And keep your hand away from your sword. The bridge remembers the last blood spilled here. It remembers the Fenwood horses crossing from the east. It remembers the screams.”

Theme & Atmosphere

Vhasia is a realm of broken coronations and split loyalties, still bleeding from a wound a decade old. Once a proud province of the Utaran empire, the unified Sun Court shattered like its heraldic sunburst when the Fenwoods of Viterra and their allies crossed the Belworth, sacked the capital of Lence, and cast down the old Blood line. Duke Braedyn Fenwood—uncle of Veralyn, now the Warrior Queen of Viterra—led the assault, his ironshod cavalry grinding the Sun King's army into the mud of the Ford of Ashes. They burned Lence until the mirrors melted and the very stones wept. They left the throne empty—not destroyed, but open. And they left a wound that has not healed.

But the wound runs deeper than smoke and stone. Forty years ago, during the Province Wars, King Wymund of Viterra sought alliance with Vhasia through marriage—taking Elianor Everblood (née Longblood) as his queen. She bore him two daughters, his heir Valdais Longblood, and a second son, Wyllmot, who was made heir to the Viterran throne. When Wymund died, Elianor married his son Baldvyn II, becoming queen of Viterra again and binding the two realms in blood—but also in rumor. The jealousies between these unions ignited the provincial wars that weakened both realms, culminating in Wyllmot's clash with Valdais at Adria—a victory stained by the suspicion that brother had fought brother for a throne built on doubtful bloodlines.

When the Fenwoods came, Elianor was in her western duchy of Ethor, having married into the Vhasian royal Bloods line—a final attempt to stitch the realm back together. Her nephew had been made heir to the Sun Court on condition of that marriage, a settlement that the sack of Lence tore apart utterly. Now, Elianor rules from Ethor, her alliances woven from the threads of three marriages and the quiet terror that none of her sons may be truly “hers” by the blood that matters. She is not mourning. She is waiting—for proof, for a sign, for the moment when the fractured sun might finally cast a single shadow.

The memory of the sack haunts every negotiation. The Queen-Mother, Elianor Everblood, lost her third husband—the old Sun King—and her cousins and sisters in the flames. Her second son, Wyllmot, King of Viterra and Duke of Aberiel, died fighting rebels in Viterra, leaving his crown to his daughter, Veralyn—who is now the Warrior Queen, who was the Fenwoods' ward, and who was there when they burned Lence. Elianor rules from a riverside keep, weaving alliances and hiding a secret—a Dauphin, perhaps her grand-nephew, perhaps a changeling, who may be the key to restoring the Sun Court. Her eldest son, Valdais Longblood, Duke of Adria in the west, claims the throne by blood and steel, but Elianor does not trust him. She has outlived three husbands and two sons. She is not mourning. She is waiting.

The Fenwood sack was not just a military defeat; it was a betrayal of chivalry itself. The knights of Vhasia had sworn to protect the realm with the Round of Five Virtues: Courage, Courtesy, Mercy, Prowess, Truth. At Lence, the Fenwoods showed no mercy. The knights still argue about whether chivalry died there or whether it was already a corpse, dressed in armour, a thin veneer over the cruelty of the world. The Cantor Alain sings the Planctus Armatus each spring, and the congregation weeps for Sir Corin, who unbuckled his gorget to hear a thief's voice and was undone by the mercy he offered. Sir Corin died at Lence. He was trying to save a child. The child is now the Dauphin—or so the Queen-Mother claims—and perhaps the living proof that Elianor's bloodline, however tangled, still holds the true sun.

What you notice first: The smell of old smoke and fresh ink. The way every noble's seal has a hairline crack—like the fault lines in old marriage alliances. The constant, nervous laughter of courtiers who remember whose head rolled last season—and whose mother married which king. The way the knights touch their gorgets when someone speaks of mercy, remembering Sir Corin's unbuckling at Lence—and the queen who watched it all from Ethor, wondering if the mercy she'd been shown was ever real.

The one rule that kills newcomers: Never carry a coin that bears only one sunburst. The realm has three mints, and the wrong metal on the wrong road is treason. Also, never speak the name “Fenwood” without naming Queen Veralyn first. The wound is still raw. The Queen-Mother's spies are everywhere—and they have long memories. And never forget: the first treason was not in the field, but in the nursery, when a queen wondered if the son in her cradle was truly the king's.

Regional Mood: Crowns, Courts, and the Weight of Lence

Here, the past is a weapon and the future is a weight—not of silver, but of memory. The Fenwoods came from Viterra across the Belworth with fire and iron, broke the Sun King's army at the Ford of Ashes, and burned Lence until the mirrors melted. Duke Braedyn Fenwood led them; his daughter Veralyn—now the Warrior Queen of Viterra—was her father's attendant and witness to the sack, a fact that complicates every border negotiation. The Vhasian nobility has not forgotten. They cannot forgive. But they cannot afford a war with Viterra, either.

So they fight each other instead.

Now every duke with a long purse and a short conscience backs a claimant. The Queen-Mother weaves alliances from her riverside keep in the west, holding a Dauphin—perhaps her grand-nephew, perhaps a commoner with a convincing birthmark—as a bargaining chip. The Two Crowns—Valdais Longblood of Adria in the west, and a rival claimant backed by the southern parlements—march their levies. Routiers sell their swords by the season, and the memory of Lence is ground into every negotiation. Vhasia is not a nation at war. It is a nation that forgot how to be at peace. And ten years later, the smoke from Lence still hasn't cleared—and neither has the doubt about whose blood truly flows in the veins of those who claim the throne.

The Ninth Taboo Manifestation

In any Vhasian court, the ninth citation of precedent voids all previous. The wise stop at eight and settle. Those who cite the ninth are said to hear a faint laughter from the marble walls—and their cases are never heard again. The laughter is the dead king's. He was the ninth king of his line. He died at Lence. He is still laughing—because the ninth citation is the only one that names the Fenwoods as the true heirs. No one has ever cited it. No one dares.

Also, the ninth cup poured at a feast is for the Hollow. The wise pour eight and leave the ninth empty. Those who pour the ninth see a pale figure standing at the high table—waiting to collect a weight. The figure is the dead king, checking his oath-stick.

The Province Wars (Forty Years Ago)

Before the Fenwood sack, there was the Province Wars—a generation of low-intensity raiding between the cantons that left the old crown weakened and the knights bitter. The peace that ended the Province Wars was a treaty of exhaustion, not reconciliation. It was during those wars that the dynastic tangles began: King Wymund of Viterra married Elianor Everblood to seal the peace, only for her to later wed his son Baldvyn II after Wymund's death—a union that sparked the very conflicts it was meant to prevent. The veterans of the Province Wars are now the old men who watch the new claimants with weary eyes. They remember when mercy meant something. They also remember when it cost everything—and they whisper that the first casualty of those wars was trust in the marriage beds of queens.

The Players of the Great Game

The succession crisis has four major factions, each with a claim, a secret, and a price—each shadowed by the legacy of dual marriages and uncertain parentage.

  • Queen-Mother Elianor Everblood (née Longblood): Widow of three kings—Wymund of Viterra, his son Baldvyn II, and the last Sun King. Mother of Valdais and the late Wyllmot. She rules the western canton of Ethor. Her price is a guarantee that her grand-nephew (the Dauphin) will not be killed. Her secret: she is not sure if Valdais is truly her son by Wymund—or if he was conceived during the turbulent shift between husbands. She acts as if he is of the Blood, because certainty would destroy what remains of the Longblood line—and because the Dauphin may be her only hope for an heir without doubt.
  • Duke Valdais Longblood of Adria: The western claimant. Nephew—or half-brother, depending on the rumor—to the last Sun King. A tournament champion with a flawless record and a suspicious habit of opponents falling ill before jousts. His claim rests on blood and steel. His secret: his mother, Elianor, does not trust him—and he suspects why. He was not at Lence. He wishes he had been—so he could have died with honor instead of living with the question: “Whose son am I, truly?”
  • The Two Crowns (Rival Dukes of the South and East): Backed by the southern parlements and the eastern march lords who face the Belworth. Their claim rests on election and expediency. Their secret: they have been known to accept Viterran gold—but never the Fenwood seal. They whisper that Veralyn is not her father's daughter. They are wrong. They are also afraid—afraid that if the Viterran line is false, then the Vhasian claim via Elianor's marriages is equally doubtful, and the realm has no true sun left to claim.
  • The Last Dauphin (The False/Real Heir): A child of nine years, hidden in the Queen-Mother's keep. His legitimacy is a matter of faith. His existence is the only thing keeping Valdais from marching on his mother's castle. His secret: even Elianor does not know if he is truly her grand-nephew—but she has dreamed of Sir Corin, who died saving a child at Lence. The child in the dream has the Dauphin's face—and perhaps, just perhaps, the unbroken line of Elianor's blood, however it flowed.

The Cantor Alain and the Planctus Armatus

No figure embodies Vhasia's tragedy more than Alain, the Iron Bard. A cantor who survived the sack of Lence as a boy—rescued by Sir Corin himself—Alain carries a cracked ring and a voice that carries the weight of the dead. Each dusk, he sings the Planctus Armatus (the Lament for the Armoured), a song that recounts Corin's unbuckling and the mercy that killed him. The song has eight verses. The ninth is silence—the space where the Hollow listens. The fourth verse, oft-whispered, speaks of “the queen who wore two crowns, and the son who knew not which father's blood he carried.”

Mechanical Hook: Once per session, a character who hears Alain sing (or who recites a verse of the Planctus Armatus) may gain +1 die on a roll involving mercy or witness. On a failure, they mark 1 Fatigue—the weight of remembering. If they recite the fourth verse specifically, they may instead gain +1 Effect on a roll involving inheritance or legitimacy—but mark 1 Corruption, as the doubt it stirs is a poison to certainty.

Prominent NPCs of Vhasia

|p0.25|p0.3|p0.15|}

Name

Role/TitleMotivationRumoured Quirk
Elianor EverbloodQueen-Mother of EthorRestore her grand-nephew (real or false) to the throne—and prove, at last, whose blood truly flows in Valdais's veins. She has outlived three husbands and two sons. She is not mourning—she is planning, and waiting for the moment the doubt ends. Her shadow has begun to drift independently—sometimes toward Ethor, sometimes toward the memory of Wymund's court, sometimes toward the empty space where Baldvyn II's queen should stand.
Valdais LongbloodDuke of Adria, western claimantUnite Vhasia under a strong western crown—and silence the whispers about his parentage once and for all. He has never lost a joust. His opponents have a habit of falling ill before the tilt. He does not poison them. His mother's spies do. He carries a shard of Wymund's coronation sword in his boot—a reminder that the first throne he ever knew may not have been his by right.
The Red MarshalExiled commander of the Sun Court's armyWin back Lence—or die tryingHe lost the Battle of Ashes; he still sees it in his dreams and his nightmares. He was the last man to see the old king alive. The king's last words were a curse on the Fenwood name. He wonders, in the quiet hours, if the curse was meant for the Fenwoods—or for the queens who married twice and left the realm questioning whose sons were truly theirs.
Seint Michel's JusticiarMagistrate of the Order of the Gilded ScaleEnforce the chivalric code without mercyHe carries a scale with one pan empty; the empty pan is for lies. He has never been able to balance it. The empty pan holds the ghost of Sir Corin—and sometimes, he swears, the whisper of Elianor's doubt as she watched the sack from Ethor.
Sister OriaCustodian of the Lantern-Rites House on the BelworthKeep the rites of hospitality and witnessShe has never drawn a blade. She has never needed to. The Queen-Mother's enemies who cross her threshold have a habit of forgetting why they came. Her scroll records every name and every death of those who fell at Lence.
The Cantor AlainIron Bard, bearer of the Cracked RingSing the truth of Corin's mercy before the armour closes again—and sing the truth of the queens who bore sons to two kings, and the sons who never knew which father's blood they carried. Feathers grow from his temples; his shadow moves a half-step behind him. His songs are the only honest history left in Vhasia. He sings the names of the dead every dusk. The dead listen—and sometimes, they answer.
The Dauphin (Child)A child of nine, hidden in the Queen-Mother's keepSurvive—and perhaps, one day, answer the question that has haunted Elianor since Valdais was in swaddling clothes. He has never seen the sun. He knows three languages, the lineage of every noble house, and the taste of every poison. He does not know if he is a prince or a puppet. He dreams every night of a knight with an unbuckled gorget—and of a queen who wore two crowns, wondering which throne was truly hers.

Names of Vhasia

Inspired by the fractured duchies: Francien, Norman, and Brittonic influences. Soft vowels, silent consonants, and surnames that recall a bridge, a ford, or a stained banner. Many names echo the Province Wars era, when marriage alliances were made and broken.

(Château/Cathedral/Forest)

Wayside shrine with pilgrims tacking petitions to a scorched sun disc; vine-terrace clos above a millstream with napping watchmen. The oldest vintners whisper that the “Crownbreaker” vintage was blessed by Elianor herself—once as Viterran queen, once as Vhasian queen—before the province wars turned the gift to ash.
